title: Aliens: Fireteam Elite

shutdown.note: Announced to become completely unplayable — co-op campaign included — on August 5, 2026.

notes: A paid co-op campaign that dies with its servers: the exact failure mode the Stop Killing Games movement exists to stop.

title: NHL 22

shutdown.note: Online modes end August 31, 2026 on all consoles; offline exhibition play survives.

notes: EA's Service Updates page is a rolling obituary column — NHL 22 and NHL 23 die the same day.

shutdown.note: Online modes end August 31, 2026 — under four years after a $70 launch.
Server shutdown announced for later in 2026; offline racing will remain.

title: NBA 2K25

shutdown.note: 2K's standard ~2-year server sunset is scheduled; MyCareer and MyTeam progress dies with it.

notes: Career-mode saves live on 2K's servers, so the shutdown deletes single-player progress too.

title: Fortnite
notes: The biggest game on earth exists at Epic's pleasure. Thousands of dollars of skins per account, zero survival plan.

title: Apex Legends
notes: The Nintendo Switch version is already being sunset in August 2026 — platform by platform is how live games usually start to die.

title: Call of Duty: Warzone
notes: The original Warzone map was already retired once (Caldera, 2023), and Warzone Mobile was killed in 2026. Precedent set.

title: Call of Duty: Black Ops 6
notes: Even the single-player campaign requires a connection (asset streaming), and the disc is mostly a download stub. $70 for a game you can't play in your own house without Activision's blessing.

title: Destiny 2
notes: Bungie already deleted paid campaigns from the live game (the 'Content Vault', 2020) without refunds. You don't even own the parts you're still allowed to play.

title: Genshin Impact
notes: A single-player-feeling world that is 100% server-side — and some players have spent five figures on it. PS4 support already ended in 2026.

title: League of Legends
notes: Too big to fail — until it isn't. Structurally, seventeen years of purchases evaporate the day Riot decides.

title: Valorant
notes: Always-online, kernel-level anti-cheat, all cosmetics server-side.

title: Counter-Strike 2
notes: Community servers and offline bots soften the risk — but remember what happened to CS:GO, which this game overwrote.

title: Overwatch 2
notes: This game exists because Blizzard deleted the last one. Owners of Overwatch 1 know exactly what the end-of-life plan is: overwrite and move on.

title: Marvel Rivals
notes: Licensed-IP live service: when the Marvel license or the revenue ends, so does the game. NetEase laid off part of the team while it was a hit.

title: The Finals
notes: PS4 service already sunset in 2026. Nexon killed LawBreakers in 13 months and KartRider after 19 years.

title: ARC Raiders
notes: You paid $40 for an extraction shooter that is 100% server-side, with no stated end-of-life plan.

title: Helldivers 2
notes: Even solo missions run through Sony's servers. In 2024 Sony tried to retroactively require PSN accounts — briefly making the game unbuyable in 177 countries — and backed down only after 200k negative reviews.

title: Diablo IV
notes: A $70 game where playing the campaign alone still requires Blizzard's servers. Diablo III on console proved offline is possible; Blizzard chose not to.

title: Escape from Tarkov
notes: Up to $250 editions, all of it server-side.

title: Dead by Daylight
notes: Hundreds of dollars of DLC killers and cosmetics per dedicated player, all dependent on Behaviour's servers.

title: Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege X
notes: A decade of purchases sits on the servers of the publisher that killed The Crew and revoked its licenses.

title: Tom Clancy's The Division 2
notes: The whole campaign — solo included — runs on Ubisoft servers.

title: Skull and Bones
notes: Always-online even sailing solo. A $70 'quadruple-A' game with The Crew's publisher and The Crew's architecture.

title: Gran Turismo 7
notes: Campaign progress saves to Sony's servers; offline you get a fraction of the game. GT Sport already showed how this ends — its shutdown gutted the game in 2024.

title: EA Sports FC 26
notes: Ultimate Team — where the money and most players are — dies when EA flips the switch, as it has for every FIFA before it, on a ~3-year schedule. Money spent on packs is never coming back.

title: Madden NFL 26
notes: Annual-cycle server shutdowns are written into the business model.

title: Fall Guys
notes: Went free-to-play under Epic in 2022; people who bought it got some credits. Epic refunded Paragon and Rumbleverse when it killed them — better record than most.

title: Rocket League
notes: Offline splitscreen and exhibition still work. Epic already ended Mac/Linux support and delisted the paid version — the perimeter shrinks over time.

title: Roblox
notes: Kids' allowances by the billions, converted into a server-side currency for experiences that can vanish at moderation's or the market's whim.

title: World of Warcraft
notes: Two decades of characters live on Blizzard's servers. Blizzard has fought private servers in court — there is no sanctioned afterlife.

title: Warframe
notes: A beloved operator, but structurally the same deal: twelve years of player investment, no offline plan.

title: Elden Ring Nightreign
notes: Co-op-centric spin-off; solo offline play exists but the designed experience needs matchmaking servers.

title: Monster Hunter Wilds
notes: Hunts work offline; the hub, events and co-op don't. Capcom has also retrofitted DRM onto old games — your copy can change under you.

title: Street Fighter 6
notes: Offline versus and arcade survive a shutdown; Battle Hub and rank don't. Capcom's older fighters all lost their online modes eventually.

title: Mortal Kombat 1
notes: Offline fighting survives; unlocks and seasonal content are server-side. WB killed MultiVersus and MK Onslaught within the last two years.

title: Halo Infinite
notes: The campaign installs and plays offline; multiplayer and everything you bought in it is server-side. Older Halo games' 360 servers were shut in 2022.

title: Forza Horizon 5
notes: Horizon 1 through 4 have all been delisted on schedule as licenses expire. This one's clock is already ticking.

title: Forza Motorsport (2023)
notes: Career progression required online connectivity at launch — a racing game you can't fully play in a tunnel.

title: Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024
notes: The world itself streams from Azure. Offline mode exists but is a husk of what you paid for. When these servers sunset, so does the product as sold.

title: Rust
notes: Community servers are first-class: even if Facepunch vanished, the game wouldn't.

title: Among Us
notes: Local wifi play exists — a rare survival hatch in a multiplayer-first game.

title: Splatoon 3
notes: The single-player campaign lives on the cartridge forever; the online core dies whenever Nintendo repeats what it did to the Wii U and 3DS in 2024.

title: Grand Theft Auto V
notes: Story mode is yours offline. GTA Online already died once — on PS3/360 in 2021 — and everything bought there died with it.

title: Red Dead Redemption 2
notes: The campaign is safe. Red Dead Online was abandoned years ago — a live service can die of neglect, too.

title: Borderlands 4
notes: Fully playable offline solo — Gearbox confirmed it after the Borderlands 3 launch-era backlash taught them the question matters.

title: Baldur's Gate 3
notes: DRM-free on GOG, offline-complete, physical deluxe edition with the game on disc. This is what respecting a customer looks like.

title: Cyberpunk 2077
notes: CD Projekt sells it DRM-free on its own store. Buy it once, back it up, keep it forever.

title: Elden Ring
notes: Online co-op and messages are optional garnish; the game is complete offline and complete on disc.

title: The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom
notes: The complete game ships on the cartridge — increasingly the exception, even at Nintendo, as Switch 2 'game-key cards' spread.

title: Mario Kart World
notes: The $80 flagship ships complete on cartridge. Check the box on other Switch 2 games: 'game-key card' means you're buying a download code in a case.

title: Mario Kart 8 Deluxe
notes: Offline play is forever. Online play lives exactly as long as Nintendo wants — ask Mario Kart Wii players, cut off in 2014.

title: God of War Ragnarök
notes: Fully offline — but the PC version added a mandatory PSN account login, a leash Sony now attaches to its single-player ports.

title: Death Stranding 2: On the Beach
notes: The social strand features need servers; the journey doesn't.

title: Starfield
notes: Plays fully offline — but the 'physical' premium edition was a code in a box. No disc preserves this game.

title: Doom: The Dark Ages
notes: A fully offline campaign wrapped in Denuvo anti-tamper — durability by design, undermined by DRM.

title: Redfall
notes: Launched requiring a connection even solo; after the studio was closed, the final update made it playable offline forever. The bar every dying game should clear.

title: Age of Empires II: Definitive Edition
notes: A 26-year-old game still thriving — skirmish and LAN survive anything.

title: Minecraft
notes: Offline worlds and self-hosted servers make it nearly unkillable — though Microsoft's migration to mandatory accounts showed who holds the keys.

title: The Sims 4
notes: Plays offline — good, because EA just killed The Sims Mobile and has $1,000+ of DLC riding on this one.

title: Battlefield 6
notes: EA switched off Battlefield Hardline's console servers in June 2026 and has killed every older Battlefield's online eventually. The campaign will outlive the part everyone actually buys it for.

title: Star Wars Jedi: Survivor
notes: Offline single-player (Denuvo was removed post-launch). The disc still needs a hefty download.

title: Split Fiction
notes: Couch co-op means the whole game works with zero servers — Hazelight builds them like they used to.

title: Dragon Age: The Veilguard
notes: Fully offline, no EA account required — BioWare's other 2020s game, Anthem, is already in the graveyard for doing the opposite.

title: Assassin's Creed Shadows
notes: Playable offline after activation — but Ubisoft Connect sits between you and your game, from the publisher that decommissioned online services for 90+ titles in one sweep.

title: The Crew Motorfest
notes: Same architecture that killed The Crew — but after the lawsuit and backlash, Ubisoft pledged offline modes for Motorfest and The Crew 2. Pressure works; hold them to it.

title: Hogwarts Legacy
notes: Fully offline open world; Denuvo is the only string attached to the PC version.

title: Suicide Squad: Kill the Justice League
notes: Launched always-online even solo; its final update added a full offline mode. The right ending to the wrong design — and proof patches like this cost publishers little.

title: Kingdom Come: Deliverance II
notes: DRM-free on GOG. A 100-hour RPG nobody can repossess.

title: S.T.A.L.K.E.R. 2: Heart of Chornobyl
notes: DRM-free on GOG.

title: Alan Wake 2
notes: Digital-only for its first year; the eventual deluxe disc still needs a download. A fully offline game you can't fully hold.

title: Wayfinder
notes: Launched as an always-online live service; when its publisher walked away, the developers bought it back and rebuilt it fully offline rather than kill it. The counter-example to every tombstone in the graveyard.

title: No Man's Sky
notes: A decade of free updates and the whole universe still works offline.

title: Deep Rock Galactic
notes: Solo play with Bosco works offline. Rock and stone.

title: Stardew Valley
notes: DRM-free on GOG, complete on cartridge, updated free for a decade by one person. The anti-live-service.

title: Factorio
notes: DRM-free download from the developer's own site. Never goes on sale; never gets taken away.

title: Hollow Knight: Silksong
notes: DRM-free on GOG. $20, offline forever.
